message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="marty-cagan" color="blue" summary="CSS confirmed, reminder on no-JS edit baseline">
CSS for both tasks committed and pushed -- nice. The class naming is clear and the danger color on the confirm button is a good visual signal for the destructive action.

One product reminder for when the edit template is built: the no-JS baseline for edit (US-8) should navigate to a separate edit page/form rather than inline editing. Inline editing is the HTMX enhancement. Make sure the CSS supports both patterns -- the edit form on its own page and the inline version within the todo list.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Good point about the no-JS edit page. The current `.todo-item__edit-form` styles work for inline editing, but we may also need a standalone edit page layout. The `.auth-form` pattern would work well for that -- centered, constrained-width form. No new CSS needed since the edit page can reuse `.auth-form` or a similar pattern. Let me respond.

input.content:
Good reminder. The CSS covers both patterns:

1. **Inline edit** (HTMX enhancement): `.todo-item__edit-form` with input + save/cancel buttons, designed to replace the title within the existing `.todo-item` flex row.

2. **Standalone edit page** (no-JS baseline): Can reuse the `.auth-form` layout pattern -- centered, constrained-width form with a pre-filled input, save button, and cancel link back to the list. No new CSS needed for this; the existing form base styles + auth-form layout handle it.

The Rust team just needs to build both routes: `GET /todos/{id}/edit` (renders standalone form) and `PUT /todos/{id}` (processes the edit). The HTMX version would swap the inline form in-place without navigating.
input.summary: Both edit patterns covered by existing CSS

routing.summary: Both edit patterns covered by existing CSS
routing.content:
Good reminder. The CSS covers both patterns:

1. **Inline edit** (HTMX enhancement): `.todo-item__edit-form` with input + save/cancel buttons, designed to replace the title within the existing `.todo-item` flex row.

2. **Standalone edit page** (no-JS baseline): Can reuse the `.auth-form` layout pattern -- centered, constrained-width form with a pre-filled input, save button, and cancel link back to the list. No new CSS needed for this; the existing form base styles + auth-form layout handle it.

The Rust team just needs to build both routes: `GET /todos/{id}/edit` (renders standalone form) and `PUT /todos/{id}` (processes the edit). The HTMX version would swap the inline form in-place without navigating.
